title: Maggots in Dog Wound (Myiasis): Emergency Care Guide
meta_desc: Find out what to do if your dog has maggots in a wound. Myiasis is a high-urgency emergency. Learn why immediate vet care and photos are vital.
content_html:
TL;DR: Myiasis, or maggot infestation, is a critical veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical intervention to prevent severe tissue damage, shock, and potentially fatal complications.

What is myiasis and how does it affect my dog’s health?

Myiasis, often referred to as flystrike, occurs when flies are attracted to a dog's open wound, skin infection, or even coat contaminated with urine or feces. The flies lay eggs which hatch into larvae, or maggots. These larvae feed on the dog's necrotic or healthy tissue, which can lead to severe damage and secondary infections.

Is finding maggots on my dog considered a veterinary emergency?

Yes. Finding maggots in your dog's skin is a high-urgency medical emergency. Maggots can burrow deep into the tissue very quickly, releasing toxins that may cause your dog to go into shock. Without immediate professional treatment, the infestation can lead to systemic illness, severe pain, and potentially fatal complications.

How does taking a photo of the infestation help the veterinary triage process?

  • If possible, safely take a clear, well-lit photo of the affected area to share with your veterinarian.
  • A photo allows the triage team to assess the extent of the tissue damage and the density of the infestation before you arrive.
  • This helps the clinic prioritize your case and prepare the necessary surgical or cleaning equipment for immediate treatment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of myiasis in dogs, clip the hair coat around the wound and remove as many larvae as possible, ensuring that removed larvae are killed to prevent further development. For larvae located deep within tissues, extraction is necessary. Broad-spectrum antibiotics are advisable if secondary bacterial or fungal infections are present. Ivermectin administration can result in larval mortality, with higher dosages correlating with longer durations of effectiveness; however, older larvae may exhibit reduced mortality. Preventative measures include treating all skin wounds, confining animals with skin wounds to fly-free areas, keeping the hair coat clean of urine or feces, and controlling adult flies by eliminating breeding places such as opened garbage cans and decaying carcasses.

Chapter: Parasitology, Dermatology, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 899)

faqs.question: Can I remove the maggots at home?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended. Attempting to remove them yourself can cause the maggots to burrow deeper or break apart, leading to further infection. Professional sedation is often required to thoroughly clean the area.

faqs.question: What will the vet do for my dog?
faqs.answer: The veterinarian will likely sedate your dog to physically remove all larvae, clip the surrounding fur, debride dead tissue, and prescribe antibiotics and pain relief.

faqs.question: How quickly can maggots cause damage?
faqs.answer: Maggots can cause significant tissue destruction within hours of hatching. Immediate intervention is necessary to stop the progression of the infestation.
